title: Soil Science & Building Fertility
pillar_name: Regenerative Food Systems, Animal Stewardship & Land Care

required.title: [Equipment] Soil pH test kit or pH meter

suggested.title: [Video] Dr. Elaine Ingham's Soil Food Web courses

supplementary.title: [For Reference] Your local Cooperative Extension Service

competence_markers.description: The student can perform a jar test and ribbon test and accurately describe their soil's texture
The student can test soil pH and explain what the reading means for nutrient availability
The student can describe the major components of the soil food web and their roles
The student manages a compost system that produces finished compost meeting quality standards
The student has planted and terminated at least one cover crop
The student can identify at least five plant indicator species and explain what each indicates
The student has sent soil samples to a lab, interpreted the results, and designed an amendment plan
The student maintains soil records (pH readings, test results, amendments applied) over time

content.why_this_matters:
You already know that soil is alive. You have held healthy soil in your hands, counted earthworms, built compost, and watched mulch protect the ground. Now you are going to learn the science behind what you observed — and use that science to build soil deliberately, on purpose, with measurable results.

This matters because soil is the foundation of every food-producing system in 2.5. You cannot grow a productive garden, manage an orchard, or run livestock on pasture without understanding what your soil is made of, what it needs, and how to give it what it needs. The farmer who guesses about soil is gambling with every season. The farmer who tests, interprets, and amends is making informed decisions that compound over years.

You will learn to test soil, read the results, design amendment plans, manage compost as an engineered system, plant cover crops, and read the landscape through the plants that grow on it. By the end of this packet, you will know more about soil than most adults — including most people who call themselves gardeners.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Soil texture analysis** — performing jar tests and ribbon tests to determine sand/silt/clay composition
2. **Soil pH understanding** — testing pH, understanding its effect on nutrient availability, and knowing how to adjust it
3. **The soil food web** — understanding the major organisms and their roles in nutrient cycling
4. **Composting as engineering** — managing C:N ratio, temperature, moisture, and aeration to produce quality compost
5. **Cover crop management** — selecting, planting, and terminating cover crops for specific soil-building goals
6. **Plant indicator reading** — interpreting what wild plant communities reveal about soil conditions
7. **Soil test interpretation** — sending samples to a lab, reading reports, and designing amendment plans
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Soil Structure and Texture — The Jar Test and Ribbon Test

Soil is made of mineral particles in three size categories: sand (largest, gritty), silt (medium, silky), and clay (smallest, sticky). The proportions determine your soil's texture, which affects everything: drainage, nutrient holding capacity, workability, and what grows well.

**The Jar Test:**
1. Fill a quart mason jar one-third full with soil from your garden or field. Remove rocks, roots, and debris.
2. Fill the jar with water to near the top. Add a tablespoon of dish soap (helps separate particles).
3. Shake vigorously for two minutes. Set on a flat surface.
4. After one minute, mark the level of settled material on the jar with tape or marker. This is your sand layer.
5. After two hours, mark again. The new material above the sand is silt.
6. After 24–48 hours, the water should be mostly clear. The material above the silt line is clay.
7. Measure each layer. Calculate the percentage of each: sand, silt, clay.

**What the results mean:**
- High sand (>60%): soil drains fast, dries quickly, holds few nutrients. You need organic matter to improve water and nutrient retention.
- High clay (>40%): soil holds water and nutrients well but compacts easily, drains poorly, and is hard to work when wet. You need organic matter to improve structure and drainage.
- Balanced (roughly 40% sand, 40% silt, 20% clay): this is loam — the ideal garden soil. Most soils are not loam. The goal of soil building is to move toward loam-like behavior by adding organic matter.

**The Ribbon Test:**
Take a golf-ball-sized piece of moist soil. Roll it into a ball. Then squeeze it between your thumb and forefinger, pushing it out into a flat ribbon. How long a ribbon can you make before it breaks?
- Less than 1 inch: sandy soil
- 1–2 inches: loamy soil
- More than 2 inches: clay soil

These tests give you baseline information about your soil's physical properties. They are the starting point, not the whole picture.

### Part 2: Soil pH and Nutrient Availability

pH measures how acidic or alkaline your soil is, on a scale from 0 (extremely acidic) to 14 (extremely alkaline). Most garden plants grow best between pH 6.0 and 7.0. Most soils in the eastern United States are naturally acidic (below 7.0); most soils in the arid West are naturally alkaline (above 7.0).

**Why pH matters more than you think:** pH determines which nutrients are actually available to plants. Your soil may contain abundant iron, manganese, or phosphorus — but if pH is wrong, plants cannot access them. A soil test might show plenty of a nutrient, yet plants show deficiency symptoms because pH has locked it away.

- **Below 6.0:** Iron, manganese, and aluminum become more available (sometimes toxically so). Phosphorus, calcium, and magnesium become less available. Bacterial activity slows.
- **6.0–7.0:** The sweet spot for most nutrients and most soil biology. Maximum availability of the widest range of nutrients.
- **Above 7.5:** Iron, manganese, zinc, and phosphorus become less available. Calcium and magnesium dominate.

**Adjusting pH:**
- To raise pH (make less acidic): apply agricultural lime (calcium carbonate). The amount depends on your current pH and soil type — clay soils need more lime than sandy soils to achieve the same change.
- To lower pH (make less alkaline): apply elemental sulfur or acidifying fertilizers. This is slower and harder than raising pH.

Test your soil's pH. Record it. This is a number you will track over years.

### Part 3: The Soil Food Web

The soil beneath your feet contains a community of organisms that rivals any ecosystem on Earth in complexity.

**Bacteria** are the most numerous. A teaspoon of healthy soil contains 100 million to 1 billion bacteria. They decompose organic matter, releasing nutrients in forms plants can absorb. Some bacteria (rhizobia) form partnerships with legume roots and fix atmospheric nitrogen into plant-available form — free fertilizer from the air.

**Fungi** form vast networks of threads (hyphae) through the soil. Mycorrhizal fungi form direct partnerships with plant roots: the fungus extends the plant's root network by hundreds of times, accessing water and nutrients (especially phosphorus) the roots alone could never reach. In return, the plant feeds the fungus sugars from photosynthesis. This is not a metaphor — it is a literal physical connection between fungal threads and root cells. Over 90% of plant species form mycorrhizal partnerships.

**Protozoa and nematodes** graze on bacteria. When a protozoan eats a bacterium, the nutrients locked in the bacterial cell are released in plant-available form near the root zone. This "microbial grazing" is one of the primary mechanisms by which nutrients cycle from organic matter to plants.

**Earthworms** are the soil's engineers. They physically restructure the soil, creating channels for water and air. Their castings (excrement) are extraordinarily rich in plant-available nutrients — five times the nitrogen, seven times the phosphorus, and eleven times the potassium of the surrounding soil.

**The key insight:** Soil fertility is not primarily about mineral content. It is about biological activity. A soil with moderate mineral content but thriving biology will outproduce a soil with excellent minerals but dead biology. Feed the biology, and the biology feeds the plants.

### Part 4: Composting as Engineering

At Stretching Branches, you learned that scraps become compost. Now you learn to manage the process deliberately.

**The Carbon-to-Nitrogen Ratio (C:N):**
Every organic material has a ratio of carbon to nitrogen. High-carbon materials (browns) include dried leaves, straw, cardboard, and wood chips. High-nitrogen materials (greens) include food scraps, fresh grass clippings, coffee grounds, and manure. The optimal composting ratio is approximately 25:1 to 30:1 carbon to nitrogen.

- Too much carbon (pile is mostly browns): decomposition is very slow. The bacteria do not have enough nitrogen to build their bodies.
- Too much nitrogen (pile is mostly greens): the pile smells bad (ammonia). Nitrogen is being lost to the atmosphere as gas — wasted.
- Balanced: the pile heats up within days, decomposes steadily, and produces finished compost in 2–4 months with regular turning.

**Temperature monitoring:** A properly managed hot compost pile reaches 130–160 degrees F in its core. This temperature kills most weed seeds, plant pathogens, and parasites. Use a compost thermometer (long-stemmed, costs about $20). If the pile does not heat up, it is too dry, too small (minimum 3x3x3 feet), or the C:N ratio is wrong. If it overheats (above 160 degrees F), turn it — excessive heat kills the beneficial organisms you are trying to cultivate.

**Moisture management:** The pile should feel like a wrung-out sponge. Squeeze a handful — a few drops of water should come out. Too dry: decomposition stalls. Too wet: the pile goes anaerobic (runs out of oxygen), producing methane and foul odors instead of quality compost.

**Turning:** Every 1–2 weeks, turn the pile with a fork — move the outside material to the inside. This introduces oxygen, redistributes moisture, and ensures even decomposition. A pile that is never turned will still decompose, but slowly and unevenly.

**Finished compost looks like:** dark, crumbly, earthy-smelling material. You should not be able to identify the original ingredients. If you can still see food scraps or whole leaves, it is not finished.

### Part 5: Cover Crops — Growing Soil Fertility

Cover crops are plants grown not for harvest but for what they do to the soil.

**Legumes** (clover, vetch, field peas, crimson clover) fix atmospheric nitrogen. Bacteria in nodules on their roots convert N2 gas from the air into ammonia that plants can use. When the legume is terminated (cut or tilled in), its nitrogen-rich biomass feeds the soil. A good legume cover crop can add 50–150 pounds of nitrogen per acre — for free.

**Grasses** (winter rye, oats, annual ryegrass) produce massive root systems that break up compaction, add organic matter deep in the soil profile, and prevent erosion. Their fibrous roots hold soil in place during winter rains.

**Brassicas** (tillage radish, mustard) have deep taproots that penetrate compacted layers (tillage radish can reach 2+ feet deep), and some brassicas produce compounds that suppress soil-borne diseases and nematodes (biofumigation).

**Management:** Plant cover crops when a garden bed or field will be empty — after harvest in fall, or before planting in spring. Terminate before the cover crop goes to seed (or you have created a weed problem). Termination methods: mow and till in, roll-crimp (for no-till systems), or simply cut at the base and leave as mulch.

### Part 6: Reading Plant Indicators

Experienced farmers and ecologists read the landscape through its plant communities. Wild plants do not grow randomly — they grow where conditions suit them. Learning to read these indicators gives you information about soil conditions before you ever take a soil test.

**Common indicator plants:**
- **Dock and nettles:** Rich, disturbed soil with good fertility. Often found near old barns, compost areas, or heavily manured ground.
- **Plantain (broadleaf):** Compacted soil. If plantain dominates your lawn, your soil is compacted and needs aeration.
- **Dandelions:** Compacted or acidic soil with calcium deficiency. The deep taproot is actually the plant's attempt to break through compaction and mine minerals from depth.
- **Mosses:** Low pH (acidic) soil with poor drainage and shade. Not necessarily bad — but it tells you what conditions exist.
- **Clover (appearing naturally):** Nitrogen-poor soil. Clover fixes its own nitrogen, so it thrives where nitrogen is lacking and other plants struggle.
- **Horsetail:** Acidic, poorly drained soil with high water table.

This is not folk wisdom — it is ecological observation. The plants are responding to real chemical and physical conditions.

### Part 7: Soil Testing and Interpretation

A laboratory soil test gives you data no field observation can: precise measurements of nutrient levels, pH, organic matter percentage, and cation exchange capacity.

**How to take a soil sample:**
1. Using a clean shovel or soil probe, take 10–15 samples from across the area you want to test, each 6–8 inches deep.
2. Mix all samples together in a clean bucket.
3. Remove rocks and debris. Let the mixed sample air-dry.
4. Package according to the lab's instructions (usually a pint in a bag or box).
5. Send to the lab with the appropriate form and payment.

**Reading the results:** A standard soil test reports:
- **pH** — you know what this means now
- **Organic matter percentage** — the single most important number. Below 2% is depleted. 3–5% is good. Above 5% is excellent. Each percentage point of organic matter holds approximately 20,000 gallons of water per acre.
- **Macronutrients:** Nitrogen (N), Phosphorus (P), Potassium (K) — the big three. Also Calcium (Ca), Magnesium (Mg), Sulfur (S).
- **Micronutrients:** Iron (Fe), Manganese (Mn), Zinc (Zn), Boron (B), Copper (Cu)
- **Cation Exchange Capacity (CEC):** A measure of how many nutrient particles the soil can hold. Higher CEC = more nutrient-holding capacity. Clay and organic matter have high CEC. Sand has almost none.

**Designing an amendment plan:** Based on your test results, you determine what to add. Low pH? Add lime. Low phosphorus? Add bone meal or rock phosphite. Low organic matter? Add compost and grow cover crops. Low potassium? Add greensand or wood ash. Every amendment serves a purpose based on data, not guessing.

Test your soil now. Test it again in one year. The change between tests is your evidence that your management is working — or not.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Soil Texture Analysis
**Developing well:** The student performs the jar test independently, measures layers accurately, and draws correct conclusions. "This garden has about 50% sand, so it drains fast and I need to add compost for water retention." The ribbon test produces consistent results that match the jar test.

**Developing slowly:** The student completes the tests mechanically but cannot connect texture to management implications. "It's sandy" — but no idea what that means for growing food.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have they done the test on multiple soils? A single test teaches procedure. Multiple tests teach comparison and interpretation.

### 2. pH Understanding and Testing
**Developing well:** The student tests pH accurately, can explain the significance of their reading, and knows what amendment would adjust it. They understand that pH affects nutrient availability, not just plant happiness.

**Developing slowly:** The student can read the number but treats it as abstract data with no management implication.

**Diagnostic questions:** Can they explain what happens to iron availability at high pH? Can they explain why blueberries need acidic soil? If they cannot connect pH to real plant outcomes, the concept is not yet internalized.

### 3. Soil Food Web Knowledge
**Developing well:** The student can name the major organism groups, explain what each does, and describe how they interact. They understand that feeding the biology (through compost and organic matter) is the primary path to soil fertility.

**Developing slowly:** The student memorizes organism names but cannot explain relationships. "Bacteria decompose things" without understanding the nutrient cycling mechanism.

**Diagnostic questions:** Ask: "If I till the soil repeatedly, what happens to the fungi?" or "Why would I add compost instead of synthetic fertilizer?" These application questions reveal depth versus memorization.

### 4. Compost Quality
**Developing well:** The student produces compost that is dark, crumbly, and earthy-smelling. Temperature logs show the pile reached thermophilic range (130–160 degrees F). The student can explain what they adjusted and why.

**Developing slowly:** The pile either never heated (wrong ratio, too small, too dry) or the student lost interest before completion. The end product is identifiable as partially decomposed material rather than finished compost.

**Diagnostic questions:** Did the student use a thermometer? Many compost failures go undiagnosed because temperature was never monitored. Without data, you cannot troubleshoot.

### 5. Soil Test Interpretation
**Developing well:** The student reads the lab report and can explain every line. They design an amendment plan that addresses the specific deficiencies identified. The plan includes specific products, application rates, and timing.

**Developing slowly:** The student is overwhelmed by the report or cherry-picks one number without understanding the whole picture.

**Diagnostic questions:** Sit down with the report together. Ask them to explain each section. Where they struggle, teach. Where they understand, ask them to explain the management implication.
